title: Q&A: How will the World Bank’s abandoned finance goal affect climate action? - Carbon Brief
gist: Under months of Trump-administration pressure, the World Bank scrapped its target for 45% of developing-country funding to be climate finance but indefinitely extended its climate action plan (CCAP). As the largest single provider of climate finance ($39.2bn in 2025), the bank's retreat threatens the maths of the $300bn/year Paris goal, though experts call it 'mostly a symbolic victory' for the US given strong client-country demand.

themes.theme: World Bank's scale in climate finance

insights.claim: The World Bank is the single largest provider of climate finance globally, distributing $39.2bn in 2025 alone, primarily as loans, and oversees roughly half of all MDB climate finance.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The bank's dominance means any policy shift there disproportionately moves global climate-finance flows and developing-country project funding.

insights.claim: The World Bank provided $164bn in financing with climate 'co-benefits' between 2020 and 2025, with the largest share (roughly one-fifth) going to clean energy and electricity-access projects.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Clean-energy access as the biggest allocation identifies where a pullback would bite hardest for renewable developers in emerging markets.

insights.claim: The largest recipients of World Bank climate funds since 2020 have been emerging economies Turkey ($10.3bn), India ($9bn) and Nigeria ($6.3bn), with flagship projects including nearly $3bn for Indian renewables and green hydrogen and $1.7bn for a Pakistan hydropower project.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: These concentrated recipient countries are where reduced World Bank climate lending would most directly hit project pipelines and co-financing.

insights.claim: The World Bank has been criticised for the dominance of loans that push developing countries deeper into debt, for a lack of transparency in how it classifies projects as 'climate-related', and for 'over-reporting' of climate finance.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Loan-heavy structure and questionable classification mean headline climate-finance figures may overstate real impact, a caveat for anyone underwriting against them.

insights.claim: The World Bank abandoned its target for 45% of developing-country funding to be climate finance, following months of pressure from the Trump administration, while preserving its climate action plan.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Dropping the hard target while keeping the plan is the key ambiguity that determines whether climate finance actually falls or continues on inertia.
insights.locator: The World Bank has abandoned a target

insights.claim: With climate projects reaching a 48% share of total funding in 2025, the World Bank had already exceeded its 45% target and more than doubled climate finance from $17.2bn in 2020 to $39.2bn in 2025 under the CCAP.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Because the bank had already beaten the target, dropping it may be more symbolic than operational in the near term, supporting the 'symbolic victory' read.

insights.claim: US Treasury secretary Scott Bessent called for 'jettisoning' the 45% target and wants the bank to shift from a 'myopic focus on climate' toward financing more fossil-fuel projects, including drilling for new gas, reversing the bank's 2019 commitment to stop funding upstream oil and gas.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: An explicit US push to reopen World Bank fossil-fuel lending would reshape the multilateral finance landscape for oil and gas projects if it gains board support.
insights.quote: expect the bank to immediately shift its myopic focus on climate and financing volumes to one that emphasises high-quality, durable projects.

insights.claim: In the board negotiation, 19 of 25 directors affirmed the need for both a plan and a target while the US, Russia, Kuwait and Saudi Arabia declined to sign and Japan and India abstained; the US call to drop the target was backed by Saudi Arabia and Russia.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The voting split maps the geopolitical fault lines over multilateral climate finance and signals a durable pro-climate majority among shareholders.
insights.locator: A joint statement from 19 of the 25 directors

insights.claim: The day before the CCAP was due to lapse, the World Bank dropped both the 45% target and the 35% goal it replaced while extending the CCAP, which UK development minister Jenny Chapman later confirmed was extended 'indefinitely' rather than for one year.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: An indefinite plan extension without a numeric target keeps the framework alive but removes the accountability metric, a middle ground that preserves optionality.
insights.locator: Ultimately, the day before the CCAP

themes.theme: Systemic importance to Paris finance goals

insights.claim: Roughly a fifth of the international climate finance provided and mobilised by developed countries in recent years can be attributed to their World Bank contributions, making the bank a key channel as donors cut aid budgets.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The bank lets donor nations lend far beyond their own public finance, so its posture is a leverage multiplier on the entire developed-country climate-finance effort.

insights.claim: MDBs including the World Bank have committed to $120bn in climate finance to developing countries by 2030, which would require the World Bank to increase its own climate finance around 50%, from $39.2bn today to $60bn in 2030.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Hitting the 2030 MDB pledge demands the bank scale up, not retreat, so target abandonment raises real doubt about whether that trajectory holds.

insights.claim: The maths of the $300bn-a-year Paris climate-finance goal by 2035 does not work if the MDBs pull back, which is why developed countries are taking a stand to protect the World Bank's climate role.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The $300bn target's dependence on MDB scaling makes any bank retreat a direct threat to Paris finance credibility and to the aspirational $1.3tn NCQG goal.
insights.quote: The maths of the $300bn does not work if the MDBs pull back and so I think that’s why you’re seeing developed countries taking a stand.
insights.locator: Joe Thwaites, international climate finance director

insights.claim: Some World Bank internal organisations keep their climate goals for now, including the IDA's largely grant-based funding, which retains a 45% target for its round lasting until 2028, the year of the next US presidential election.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The IDA target surviving to 2028 provides a near-term floor on grant-based climate finance and ties the outlook to the next US election cycle.
insights.locator: Some of the World Bank’s internal organisations

insights.claim: The World Bank says its climate work will remain firmly 'client driven', so future climate investment may hinge on whether recipient countries demand it rather than on donor mandates.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: A shift to demand-driven climate finance moves the decisive variable to recipient-country appetite, which polling suggests remains strong for renewables.
insights.locator: In a statement, the World Bank said

insights.claim: A 'G11+' group of World Bank shareholders representing nearly 100 developing countries, led by Brazil and China, wrote pushing back against the US framing, insisting the bank 'must remain firmly client-driven' and that countries want climate action.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Direct client-country demand for climate finance undercuts the US claim that recipients don't want it and supports continued deployment despite target loss.
insights.quote: This was the client countries [100 developing countries] saying: ‘No, we want this.’

insights.claim: ODI thinktank research found 79% of developing-country officials polled wanted MDB investment in solar projects, 54% wanted hydropower and 47% wanted wind power, while only 13% wanted investment in gas-power plants.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Overwhelming recipient preference for solar over gas directly contradicts the US fossil-financing push and signals where real project demand sits.
insights.locator: Recent research by the ODI thinktank

insights.claim: US Treasury secretary Bessent, in discussion with climate sceptic Bjørn Lomborg, characterised climate action as an 'elite belief' and argued global financial institutions should focus on growth instead.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The US reframing climate as elite versus growth is the ideological wedge shaping future MDB mandates and worth tracking for policy direction.
insights.locator: At an April event in discussion

insights.claim: Experts characterise the outcome as 'mostly a symbolic victory' for the US, but warn that major European and other shareholders should signal the bank is not 'the only game in town' and can take their money elsewhere if it caves to one shareholder.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The threat to redirect funds to other channels is the counter-leverage that could keep the bank's climate ambition intact despite US pressure.
insights.quote: If [the World Bank] are going to cave into one shareholder, when the vast majority of the other shareholders are supportive of continuing climate action, they can take their money elsewhere.
